papers.title: Ad-Click Prediction Enhanced by Nonlinear Dynamics-Inspired Feature Extraction and Ensemble Optimization
papers.summary: The primary objective of this study is to enhance the predictive performance of machine learning models used for estimating Click-Through Rate (CTR), a key metric in digital advertising analytics. Beginning with a baseline Logistic Regression (LR) model applied to the “Click-Through Rate Prediction” dataset from Kaggle, the study systematically incorporates multiple optimization layers to improve forecasting accuracy. Inspired by nonlinear dynamics concepts, new feature representations were derived from temporal patterns and textual fields using TF-IDF and Word2Vec-based embeddings. Hyperparameter optimization techniques were then applied to refine model behavior, followed by the construction of ensemble architectures combining LR, XGBoost, Random Forest (RF), and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers. Experimental results show that the optimized ensemble achieved the highest F1-score of 0.8694, yielding an improvement of approximately 12.7% over the baseline model. Overall, the study provides a comprehensive examination of feature extraction strategies, model optimization procedures, and ensemble fusion techniques, demonstrating the clear advantage of hybrid approaches in complex CTR prediction tasks.

papers.title: A Multi-Objective Machine Learning Optimization Model for Integrated Marketing Strategy and Long-Term Customer Value Management
papers.summary: This paper suggests a new multi-objective machine learning optimization framework, which converts predictive modeling, behavioral analytics, and campaign simulation to improve integrated marketing strategies and long-term customer value management. The classical marketing frameworks tend to maximize single objectives, e.g. the conversion rates without sufficiently considering the competing ones, e.g. the churn reduction, brand equity and budget efficiency. The problem in our model is formulated as a Pareto-based optimization problem and uses some advanced features such as Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks (R-GCNs), Contrastive Predictive Coding (CPC) to mine latent intent, Generative Adversarial Uplift Modeling (GAUM) to predict CLV and multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to coordinate decision-making. Experiments based on a multichannel enterprise dataset show significant improvements: CLV prediction accuracy has increased by 92.1, uplift targeting efficiency increased by 24.7 and reduction of churning by 19.2. Adversarial transfer learning increased cross domain adaptability by an average of 10.2 %. The model shows better levels of adaptability and strategic alignment in terms of acquisition, retention and personalization channels. These results confirm that causal, behavioral, and reinforcement learning with a single pipeline are effective. The framework offers an interpretation, scalable and customizable platform of the current marketing systems, which can learn and change to complex customer dynamics in real-time.
categories: International Conference on Signals and Electronic Systems
papers.source: International Conference on Signals and Electronic Systems

papers.title: MALLOC: Benchmarking the Memory-aware Long Sequence Compression for Large Sequential Recommendation
papers.summary: The scaling law, which indicates that model performance improves with increasing dataset and model capacity, has fueled a growing trend in expanding recommendation models in both industry and academia. However, the advent of large-scale recommenders also brings significantly higher computational costs, particularly under the long-sequence dependencies inherent in the user intent of recommendation systems. Current approaches often rely on pre-storing the intermediate states of the past behavior for each user, thereby reducing the quadratic re-computation cost for the following requests. Despite their effectiveness, these methods often treat memory merely as a medium for acceleration, without adequately considering the space overhead it introduces. This presents a critical challenge in real-world recommendation systems with billions of users, each of whom might initiate thousands of interactions and require massive memory for state storage. Fortunately, there have been several memory management strategies examined for compression in LLM, while most have not been evaluated on the recommendation task. To mitigate this gap, we introduce MALLOC, a comprehensive benchmark for memory-aware long sequence compression. MALLOC presents a comprehensive investigation and systematic classification of memory management techniques applicable to large sequential recommendations. These techniques are integrated into state-of-the-art recommenders, enabling a reproducible and accessible evaluation platform. Through extensive experiments across accuracy, efficiency, and complexity, we demonstrate the holistic reliability of MALLOC in advancing large-scale recommendation. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/MALLOC.

papers.title: Post-Training Fairness Control: A Single-Train Framework for Dynamic Fairness in Recommendation
papers.summary: Despite growing efforts to mitigate unfairness in recommender systems, existing fairness-aware methods typically fix the fairness requirement at training time and provide limited post-training flexibility. However, in real-world scenarios, diverse stakeholders may demand differing fairness requirements over time, so retraining for different fairness requirements becomes prohibitive. To address this limitation, we propose Cofair, a single-train framework that enables post-training fairness control in recommendation. Specifically, Cofair introduces a shared representation layer with fairness-conditioned adapter modules to produce user embeddings specialized for varied fairness levels, along with a user-level regularization term that guarantees user-wise monotonic fairness improvements across these levels. We theoretically establish that the adversarial objective of Cofair upper bounds demographic parity and the regularization term enforces progressive fairness at user level. Comprehensive experiments on multiple datasets and backbone models demonstrate that our framework provides dynamic fairness at different levels, delivering comparable or better fairness-accuracy curves than state-of-the-art baselines, without the need to retrain for each new fairness requirement. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/weixinchen98/Cofair.

papers.title: Efficient Evaluation of LLM Performance with Statistical Guarantees
papers.summary: Exhaustively evaluating many large language models (LLMs) on a large suite of benchmarks is expensive. We cast benchmarking as finite-population inference and, under a fixed query budget, seek tight confidence intervals (CIs) for model accuracy with valid frequentist coverage. We propose Factorized Active Querying (FAQ), which (a) leverages historical information through a Bayesian factor model; (b) adaptively selects questions using a hybrid variance-reduction/active-learning sampling policy; and (c) maintains validity through Proactive Active Inference -- a finite-population extension of active inference (Zrnic&Cand\`es, 2024) that enables direct question selection while preserving coverage. With negligible overhead cost, FAQ delivers up to $5\times$ effective sample size gains over strong baselines on two benchmark suites, across varying historical-data missingness levels: this means that it matches the CI width of uniform sampling while using up to $5\times$ fewer queries. We release our source code and our curated datasets to support reproducible evaluation and future research.

papers.title: HE-SNR: Uncovering Latent Logic via Entropy for Guiding Mid-Training on SWE-BENCH
papers.summary: SWE-bench has emerged as the premier benchmark for evaluating Large Language Models on complex software engineering tasks. While these capabilities are fundamentally acquired during the mid-training phase and subsequently elicited during Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), there remains a critical deficit in metrics capable of guiding mid-training effectively. Standard metrics such as Perplexity (PPL) are compromised by the"Long-Context Tax"and exhibit weak correlation with downstream SWE performance. In this paper, we bridge this gap by first introducing a rigorous data filtering strategy. Crucially, we propose the Entropy Compression Hypothesis, redefining intelligence not by scalar Top-1 compression, but by the capacity to structure uncertainty into Entropy-Compressed States of low orders ("reasonable hesitation"). Grounded in this fine-grained entropy analysis, we formulate a novel metric, HE-SNR (High-Entropy Signal-to-Noise Ratio). Validated on industrial-scale Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models across varying context windows (32K/128K), our approach demonstrates superior robustness and predictive power. This work provides both the theoretical foundation and practical tools for optimizing the latent potential of LLMs in complex engineering domains.

papers.title: ProfInfer: An eBPF-based Fine-Grained LLM Inference Profiler
papers.summary: As large language models (LLMs) move from research to production, understanding how inference engines behave in real time has become both essential and elusive. Unlike general-purpose engines such as ONNX Runtime, today's LLM inference systems offer little operator-level visibility, leaving developers blind to where time and resources go. Even basic questions -- is this workload memory-bound or compute-bound? -- often remain unanswered. To close this gap, we develop a fine-grained, non-intrusive profiling framework for modern LLM inference engines, exemplified by llama-cpp but applicable to similar runtime architectures. Built on extended Berkeley Packet Filter (eBPF) technology, our system dynamically attaches probes to runtime functions across multiple layers -- without modifying or recompiling the source. It transforms collected traces into rich visualizations of operators, graphs, timelines, and hardware counter trends, exposing how dense inference, Mixture-of-Experts routing, and operator offloading behave in practice. With less than 4% runtime overhead and high profiling fidelity, our framework makes LLM inference both transparent and diagnosable, turning performance profiling into a practical tool for optimization, scheduling, and resource-aware deployment.

papers.title: PromptGCN: Bridging Subgraph Gaps in Lightweight GCNs.
papers.summary: Graph convolutional networks (GCNs) are widely used in graph-based applications, such as social networks and recommendation systems. Nevertheless, large-scale graphs or deep aggregation layers in full-batch GCNs consume significant GPU memory, causing out-of-memory (OOM) errors on mainstream GPUs (e.g., 29-GB memory consumption on the Ogbn-products graph with five layers). The subgraph sampling methods reduce memory consumption to achieve lightweight GCNs by partitioning the graph into multiple subgraphs and sequentially training GCNs on each subgraph. However, these methods yield gaps among subgraphs, i.e., GCNs can only be trained based on subgraphs instead of global graph information, which reduces the accuracy of GCNs. In this article, we propose PromptGCN, a novel prompt-based lightweight GCN model to bridge the gaps among subgraphs. First, the learnable prompt embeddings are designed to obtain global information. Then, the prompts are attached to each subgraph to transfer the global information among subgraphs. Extensive experimental results on seven large-scale graphs demonstrate that PromptGCN exhibits superior performance compared to baselines. Notably, PromptGCN improves the accuracy of subgraph sampling methods by up to 5.48% on the Flickr dataset. Overall, PromptGCN is easily integrable with any subgraph sampling method to obtain a lightweight GCN model with higher accuracy.

papers.title: Context Meets Modality: Deep Learning Innovations for Next-Generation Recommendation Systems
papers.summary: The complexity and preferences for users have increased exponentially due to the growth and multi-modality of digital content. Users in the modern world typically deal with multiple data types, including video, audio, text, and images, which are influenced by time, place, and society. The existing techniques, namely collaborative filtering and other methods, were not effective due to the sparsity of data and also the partial awareness of the context. To avoid these drawbacks, it is essential to consider multimodal deep learning-based methods to gain awareness of the given context. New architecture models using Multi-modal approaches aim to handle any type of data sources and also based on personalized preferences of users. The developments in attention mechanisms, large language models, transformers, and graph neural networks provide opportunities for dealing with any domain and enable proper synthesis of semi-structured, structured, and unstructured data. This article aims to explore all the research works and innovations carried out in fusion models, attention, graphs, agentic models, and other related developments. In addition to the developments in the frameworks, this paper also gathers information about the explainability, trustworthiness, and fairness of the works carried out by the researchers. The third part of this work is to highlight the summary of the methods and future directions for the frameworks that can support real-time reasoning with fairness so that multimodal data can be handled along with plain text.

papers.title: Application Model of Machine Learning in Intelligent Recommendation Systems for Educational Resources
papers.summary: This paper proposes a multimodal resource recommendation algorithm (DCG-AMR) based on a dynamic cognitive graph and attention mechanism. The algorithm employs a four-layer architecture consisting of "data layer - feature layer - recommendation layer - application layer": The dynamic cognitive graph updates the user's cognitive state in real time, while a customized attention mechanism is used to assign feature weights, simultaneously achieving deep fusion of multimodal resource features. Experiments were conducted on the EdNet dataset (containing 102,000 users, 53,000 multimodal resources, and 10.86 million interaction records), comparing four algorithms: CF, CBR, NCF, and KGRec. Results show that DCG-AMR achieves Precision@10 of 82.3% (a 25.3% improvement over CF) and NDCG@15 of 81.5% (a 12.2% improvement over KGRec), with a 19.6% improvement in learning effectiveness and a 77.8% resource completion rate. Ablation experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the core module (precision drops by 7.7% after removing DCG). Stability tests show that precision fluctuates by less than 3% when the number of users varies. Scalability tests show that precision only drops by 1.2% when live streaming resources account for 100%. This algorithm effectively improves recommendation accuracy and user experience, providing support for optimizing domain-specific intelligent recommendation systems.

papers.title: Intent-Driven Product Discovery Using Natural Language Processing
papers.summary: Majority of e-commerce websites rely on search engines that are based on key words. These engines have trouble understanding the peculiarities of the human language. Consequently, irrelevant product suggestions are given to the customers who have used natural, ambiguous or casual language. This infuriates them and makes businesses spend money. A solution to this issue that is offered is a product discovery system that is driven by Artificial Intelligence and Natural Language Processing (NLP). This intelligent assistant reads the key needs of its users, suggests the most suitable products or packages, and lets the users be able to formulate their needs using their own languages. The system uses large product databases and provides relevant and personalized recommendations by combining affiliate API of popular online shopping platforms such as Amazon and Flipkart.

papers.title: Guiding the Recommender: Information-Aware Auto-Bidding for Content Promotion
papers.summary: Modern content platforms offer paid promotion to mitigate cold start by allocating exposure via auctions. Our empirical analysis reveals a counterintuitive flaw in this paradigm: while promotion rescues low-to-medium quality content, it can harm high-quality content by forcing exposure to suboptimal audiences, polluting engagement signals and downgrading future recommendation. We recast content promotion as a dual-objective optimization that balances short-term value acquisition with long-term model improvement. To make this tractable at bid time in content promotion, we introduce a decomposable surrogate objective, gradient coverage, and establish its formal connection to Fisher Information and optimal experimental design. We design a two-stage auto-bidding algorithm based on Lagrange duality that dynamically paces budget through a shadow price and optimizes impression-level bids using per-impression marginal utilities. To address missing labels at bid time, we propose a confidence-gated gradient heuristic, paired with a zeroth-order variant for black-box models that reliably estimates learning signals in real time. We provide theoretical guarantees, proving monotone submodularity of the composite objective, sublinear regret in online auction, and budget feasibility. Extensive offline experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets validate the framework: it outperforms baselines, achieves superior final AUC/LogLoss, adheres closely to budget targets, and remains effective when gradients are approximated zeroth-order. These results show that strategic, information-aware promotion can improve long-term model performance and organic outcomes beyond naive impression-maximization strategies.

papers.title: FARMARK-AI Powered Seamless Recommendation System For Farm-to-Consumers Market
papers.summary: Farmers often face challenges in selling their produce at fair prices, relying heavily on middlemen, while consumers struggle to access fresh and reliable products. Farmark is a digital platform developed to bride this gap by connecting farmers directly with consumers. The platform uses smart features like AI-assisted freshness scoring and geo-mapping to ensure that produce reaches buyers in the best conditions at fair prices. Farmers can list their products, get insights on pricing, and build credibility through ratings, while consumers can easily find nearby farms, check freshness, and make informed purchases. By simplified the farm-to-table process, Farmark not only empowers farmer but also enhances trust and convenience for consumers, offering a practical solution to the challenges in today’s agricultural supply chain.

papers.title: Delayed Feedback Modeling for Post-Click Gross Merchandise Volume Prediction: Benchmark, Insights and Approaches
papers.summary: The prediction objectives of online advertisement ranking models are evolving from probabilistic metrics like conversion rate (CVR) to numerical business metrics like post-click gross merchandise volume (GMV). Unlike the well-studied delayed feedback problem in CVR prediction, delayed feedback modeling for GMV prediction remains unexplored and poses greater challenges, as GMV is a continuous target, and a single click can lead to multiple purchases that cumulatively form the label. To bridge the research gap, we establish TRACE, a GMV prediction benchmark containing complete transaction sequences rising from each user click, which supports delayed feedback modeling in an online streaming manner. Our analysis and exploratory experiments on TRACE reveal two key insights: (1) the rapid evolution of the GMV label distribution necessitates modeling delayed feedback under online streaming training; (2) the label distribution of repurchase samples substantially differs from that of single-purchase samples, highlighting the need for separate modeling. Motivated by these findings, we propose RepurchasE-Aware Dual-branch prEdictoR (READER), a novel GMV modeling paradigm that selectively activates expert parameters according to repurchase predictions produced by a router. Moreover, READER dynamically calibrates the regression target to mitigate under-estimation caused by incomplete labels. Experimental results show that READER yields superior performance on TRACE over baselines, achieving a 2.19% improvement in terms of accuracy. We believe that our study will open up a new avenue for studying online delayed feedback modeling for GMV prediction, and our TRACE benchmark with the gathered insights will facilitate future research and application in this promising direction. Our code and dataset are available at https://github.com/alimama-tech/OnlineGMV.

papers.title: Research on Collaborative Filtering Technology for Personalized Recommendations Based on Short Videos on TikTok
papers.summary: TikTok users exceed more than 1.5 billion in 2024, with recommended lists contributing 85% of content consumption. Traditional collaborative filtering (CF) faces bottlenecks such as data sparsity (interaction matrix sparsity of 99.99%), rapid interest drift (short video lifecycle of 1.5 days), and single features. This paper proposes a dynamic collaborative filtering algorithm with multi-dimensional feature fusion (MFF-DCF). This algorithm constructs a three-dimensional feature matrix of user profile, video content, and social relationships, designs a dynamic weighting mechanism based on behavioral entropy, and introduces key features for attention enhancement. Experiments on the Douyin DY-2024-Rec dataset (100,000 users, 500,000 videos, 12 million interactions) compared the User-Based CF, Item-Based CF, and SVD algorithms. The results showed that MFF-DCF achieved a MAE of 0.42, a reduction of 35.4%, 31.1%, and 20.8% compared to the three algorithms, respectively. Precision@10 reached 0.78, an improvement of 23.8% to 50.0%. The response time was 0.32s (<1s), and the Gini coefficient was 0.23 (optimal). At 90% data sparsity, the MAE fluctuated by only 12.8%, and the Precision@10 for cold-start users reached 0.62. These results demonstrate its advantages in accuracy, real-time performance, and robustness, providing support for optimizing short video recommendations.

papers.title: Research on Model Optimization of Intelligent Recommendation System in Precision Marketing of Festival Goods
papers.summary: With the vigorous development of festival consumption market, intelligent recommendation system plays an increasingly important role in precision marketing of festival goods, but the existing recommendation model has limitations in dealing with problems such as sparse festival data and dynamic changes in user interests. This paper proposes an original festival-awareness-attention fusion recommendation algorithm (FP-AFR), which captures festival features by constructing a festival-aware module, combines the user interest modeling module and introduces the attention mechanism to achieve deep integration of festival information and user interests. The experimental simulation uses the festival commodity transaction data of an e-commerce platform to compare the FP-AFR algorithm with collaborative filtering recommendation, content-based recommendation, traditional hybrid recommendation and other algorithms. The results show that the FP-AFR algorithm has an accuracy of 82.3%, which is 15.6% higher than the collaborative filtering recommendation algorithm, the recall rate is 78.5%, which is 12.3% higher than the content-based recommendation algorithm, the F1 value is 80.4%, which is 9.8% better than the traditional hybrid recommendation algorithm, the MAE is 0.12, and the RMSE is 0.15, both lower than the comparison algorithm. The study shows that the optimization model can effectively improve the accuracy of festival commodity recommendations and provide a feasible solution for the precision marketing of festival commodities.

papers.title: Research on Model Optimization Using Deep Learning in Intelligent Employment Guidance Systems
papers.summary: This paper proposes a dynamic attention fusion employment guidance model (DAF-EGM). This model optimizes matching performance through the collaboration of multiple modules: a weighted fusion CNN with multiple convolutional kernels extracts implicit skills and collaboration requirements from job descriptions. An LSTM with a temporal decay mechanism captures dynamic trends in job seekers' career histories. A dynamic attention module is constructed by combining a time decay factor (to adapt to changes in job search demand) with a job competitiveness coefficient (to quantify market dynamics such as the number of applicants and supply-demand ratio) to achieve adaptive adjustment of feature weights. Experiments are conducted on 72,863 valid samples preprocessed from three types of real-world data: 50,000 recruitment platform data, 10,000 graduate tracking data, and supply-demand data from 12 industries. The model is compared with traditional collaborative filtering (CF), static attention deep learning (SA-DL), and CNN-RNN fusion (CR-Fusion) models. Results show that in the Top-10 recommendation scenario, DAF-EGM achieves 89.2% precision, 86.7% recall, 87.8% F1-score, and 0.083 MAE. These improvements are 26.9, 28.2, and 27.6 percentage points, respectively, compared to traditional CF, with a MAE reduction of 0.132. Compared to SA-DL, DAF-EGM achieves a 13.6 percentage point improvement in precision and a MAE reduction of 0.075. Simulation plots demonstrate that DAF-EGM's precision decreases by only 3.2% with increasing Top-K (5-25) (compared to 7.4% for traditional CF), and MAE drops below 0.1 after 15 training epochs (compared to 25 for SA-DL). Ablation experiments confirm that the time decay factor and the job competition coefficient synergistically improve F1-score by over 14 percentage points, providing technical support for the intelligent upgrade of employment guidance systems.

papers.title: Research on Interaction Design and Learning Adhesion in Large-Scale Online Practical Training Courses for Vocational Colleges: A Learning Analytics Perspective
papers.summary: To address the performance bottlenecks (response latency, high resource usage) and low learning adhesion of interactive algorithms in large-scale online practical training courses for vocational colleges, this study designs an innovative interaction algorithm based on "lightweight feature extraction + dynamic priority scheduling." This algorithm reduces redundant computations through improved PCA dimensionality reduction, enhances scenario adaptability through a "operation proficiency - task complexity" adaptation model, and balances performance and teaching effectiveness through dynamic priority scheduling. Comparative experiments were conducted with traditional collaborative filtering and static grouping algorithms in three scenarios with 100, 500, and 1,000 students based on 5,000 training data items from three majors at a vocational college. Sixty students majoring in mechanical and electrical engineering were also selected for a four-week teaching validation. Results show that in large-scale scenarios, the algorithm's recommendation accuracy reached 92.1% (an 18.3% improvement over traditional algorithms), response time was 0.482 seconds (a 62.5% reduction), and CPU utilization was 34.7% (a 44.4% reduction). During teaching validation, the experimental group's course participation rate (94.7%) and task completion rate (91.3%) increased by 24.3% and 25.9%, respectively, compared to the control group, and satisfaction scores increased by 54.8%. This research addresses the performance optimization gap in large-scale practical training algorithms for higher vocational education and provides technical support for the scaled implementation of these courses.

papers.title: Domain-Adaptive Sparse Graph Attention Network for Neural Resource Recommendation in Business Language Learning
papers.summary: We propose a Domain-Adaptive Sparse Graph Attention Network (DAS-GAT) for personalized language resource recommendation in business learning platforms, which tackles the dual challenges of computational efficiency and contextual relevance. The method integrates dynamic sparse attention mechanisms with domain-specific knowledge graphs to model relationships between learning resources, where attention weights are adaptively modulated by user proficiency levels and professional contexts. A primary advancement is found in the sparse attention variant, which strategically eliminates unimportant connections to lower computational costs without compromising recommendation accuracy. Additionally, the framework embeds knowledge graph vectors to augment resource depictions, which aligns with domain ontologies including industry-specific vocabularies. The system analyzes real-time user interaction logs and learning objectives via a temporal encoder, which produces dynamic user states that guide personalized recommendations. Experimental verification shows notable progress compared to traditional approaches, especially in managing sparse and diverse business language datasets. The proposed architecture functions as an independent service, interacting smoothly with current platform components to yield scalable and interpretable recommendations. This study improves the state-of-the-art in educational resource recommendation by merging graph neural networks with domain adaptation methods, delivering practical benefits for digital language learning ecosystems.

papers.title: Comparison of AI-generated renal diets by different large language models: a guideline-based evaluation with expert input
papers.summary: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) represents a major public health concern due to its increasing prevalence worldwide and the substantial burden it places on healthcare systems. Nutritional therapy plays a critical role in slowing the progression of the disease. Recently, large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT, Copilot, and Gemini have been introduced for dietary planning in patients with kidney disease. However, the accuracy, reliability, and guideline adherence of the diets generated by these models remain uncertain. This study aimed to evaluate AI-based dietary recommendations in light of clinical guidelines and expert opinions. Three different artificial intelligence models (ChatGPT-4o, Microsoft Copilot and Google Gemini and Microsoft Copilot) were tested using standardized patient scenarios in both Turkish and English. The models were asked to generate dietary plans for hemodialysis patients, CKD stage 3–5 patients, and a reference diet of 1800 kcal/40 g protein. The dietary outputs obtained were analyzed using the BeBIS 9 software and evaluated with IBM SPSS Statistics 26.0. Energy, macro- and micronutrient contents were compared against the TUBER 2022 (Türkiye Nutrition Guide 2022) and KDOQI (Kidney Disease Outcomes Quality Initiative) guidelines. In addition, five experts scored the models’ recommendations based on accuracy, comprehensiveness, reproducibility, innovation/personalization, and nutritional diversity/practicality. The energy values generated by all models remained below the reference targets. Significant differences were observed in energy content across models and languages (lowest: Gemini-English: 859.11 ± 151.35 kcal vs. highest: Copilot-English:1496.03 ± 249.71 kcal; p < 0.001). Potassium levels varied significantly by model (p < 0.001), with some outputs exceeding safe limits. In expert evaluations for ‘Accuracy’, Gemini-English achieved the highest score (median: 4.00), whereas ChatGPT-Turkish recorded the lowest (median: 0.00). While Gemini stood out in innovation, Copilot-Turkish plans yielded results most closely aligned with the guidelines in terms of sodium and phosphorus. None of the models fully met the guideline requirements. Although AI-based large language models hold potential for dietary planning in patients with kidney disease, they demonstrate inconsistencies in nutrient accuracy, guideline adherence, and personalization. Their standalone use in clinical practice is therefore not appropriate; expert supervision and integration with clinical guidelines are required. Not applicable.

codeBlueprint.title: LLM 表示与推荐特征融合骨架
codeBlueprint.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LLMRecFusion(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, id_dim: int, text_dim: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.id_tower = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(id_dim, 128), nn.ReLU())
        self.text_tower = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(text_dim, 128), nn.ReLU())
        self.gate = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(256, 1), nn.Sigmoid())
        self.head = nn.Linear(128, 1)

    def forward(self, id_features, llm_text_embedding):
        id_vec = self.id_tower(id_features)
        text_vec = self.text_tower(llm_text_embedding)
        gate = self.gate(torch.cat([id_vec, text_vec], dim=-1))
        fused = gate * text_vec + (1 - gate) * id_vec
        return torch.sigmoid(self.head(fused))
codeBlueprint.note: 用于验证 LLM 文本表示是否带来增益，而不是替代成熟 ID 特征链路。
